_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Toxins and Environmental: Conducted Electrical Weapon Injury (i.e., TASER). NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Conducted Electrical Weapon Injury (i.e., TASER)

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Toxins and Environmental: Conducted Electrical Weapon Injury (i.e., TASER)
points: Manage the condition that triggered the application of the conducted electrical weapon with special attention to patients meeting criterion for delirium with agitated behavior [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline ]
Ensure patient is appropriately secured or restrained with assistance of law enforcement to protect the patient and clinicians [See Agitated or Violent Patient/Behavioral Emergency Guideline]
Perform comprehensive trauma and medical assessment for injuries (e.g., from falls or altercations or concomitant medical issues)
If discharged from a distance, up to two single barbed darts (13 mm length) should be located a. Do not remove barbed dart from sensitive areas (head, neck, hands, feet, or genitals)

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Patient received either a weapon's direct-contact discharge or struck by the barbed dart of a conducted electrical weapon
Patient may have sustained fall or physical confrontation trauma
Patient may be under the influence of toxic substances and or may have underlying medical or psychiatric disorder
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
None noted

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Before removal of the barbed dart, make sure the cartridge has been removed from the conducted electrical weapon
Patient should not be restrained in the prone, face down, or hog-tied position as respiratory compromise is a significant risk
The patient may have underlying pathology before being tased (refer to appropriate guidelines for managing the underlying medical/traumatic pathology)
Perform a comprehensive assessment with special attention looking for signs and symptoms of active medical decompensation
Transport the patient to the hospital
EMS clinicians who respond for a conducted electrical weapon patient should not perform a "medical clearance" for law enforcement to then take the patient to a nonmedical facility

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: Conducted electrical weapon can be discharged in three fashions: a. Direct contact without the use of the darts

The device delivers 19 pulses per second with an average current per pulse of 2.1 milliamps which, in combination with toxins/drugs, patient's underlying diseases, excessive physical exertion, and trauma, may precipitate arrhythmias. Thus, consider cardiac monitoring and 12-lead EKG assessment
Drive Stun is a direct weapon two-point contact which is designed to generate pain and not incapacitate the subject. Only local muscle groups are stimulated with the Drive Stun technique
Thoroughly assess the patient for trauma as the patient may have fallen from standing or higher
Ascertain if more than one TASER' cartridge was used (by one or more officers, in effort to identify total number of possible darts and contacts)

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' If darts removed, document the removal location in the patient care report
' Physical exam trauma findings
' Cardiac rhythm and changes
' Neurologic status assessment findings

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Contact medical direction for clinical deterioration or interventions outside standard scope
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
